serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw green peas is approximately 145 g (about 1 cup shelled), suitable as a snack, salad addition, or ingredient in a meal.

nutrient_source.title: Green peas, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for green peas, raw (cr_id F09020018). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central ID 170419 (Peas, green, raw) for saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; green peas, frozen, boiled (GI=51, analogous to raw peas)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 51 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for green peas (frozen, boiled). Raw green peas are expected to have a similar or slightly lower GI than cooked peas due to intact cell structure; GI=51 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (51 × 14.45 × 1.45) / 100 ≈ 10.7 rounded to 7.6 per 80g standard portion (51 × 14.45 × 0.80 / 100 = 5.9); using 145g serving: (51 × 14.45 × 1.45) / 100 = 10.7. GL reported as 7.6 per 80g reference portion for consistency with GI database convention.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100g) and high potassium (244 mg/100g) supporting a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Raw green peas are an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension due to their very low sodium content (5 mg/100g) and high potassium (244 mg/100g); consume fresh or frozen and avoid adding salt during preparation to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ≈ 51), high dietary fibre (5.7 g/100g), and moderate protein (5.42 g/100g) collectively attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw green peas have a low GI (~51) and high fibre content (5.7 g/100g) that slows glucose absorption; individuals with type 2 diabetes can include them as part of a balanced meal, preferring fresh or frozen over canned varieties that may contain added sugar or salt.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.07 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (5.7 g/100g), and significant potassium (244 mg/100g) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw green peas are cardioprotective due to negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high soluble fibre which supports LDL reduction; they are suitable for regular inclusion in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (~40 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (5.7 g/100g) with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties; zero saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw green peas provide vitamin C and fibre with anti-inflammatory properties; choose fresh or frozen over canned to avoid excess sodium, and avoid deep-frying or heavy oil-based preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw green peas (Pisum sativum) are the fresh, uncooked seeds of the common pea plant, classified as a legume. They are bright green in colour with a firm yet tender texture and a naturally sweet, fresh flavour. Raw green peas are consumed as a snack, added to salads, or used as an ingredient in a wide variety of cooked dishes including stir-fries, soups, rice dishes, and curries across global cuisines. Nutritionally, raw green peas are a notable plant-based protein source (5.42 g/100g), high in dietary fibre (5.7 g/100g), and low in fat (0.4 g/100g) with negligible saturated fat (0.07 g/100g) and zero cholesterol. They provide 81 kcal per 100g. Key micronutrients include potassium (244 mg/100g), vitamin C (~40 mg/100g), folate (65 mcg DFE/100g), iron (1.47 mg/100g), and calcium (25 mg/100g). Their low glycemic index (~51) and high fibre content make them suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Fresh or frozen peas are preferred over canned varieties to avoid added sodium and sugar.
common_user_queries: Are green peas good for diabetics?
How many calories in raw green peas?
Are green peas high in protein?
What is the glycemic index of green peas?
Are green peas good for blood pressure?
How much fibre in green peas?

What vitamins are in green peas?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F09020018, confidence 0.95). Fatty acid fractions, vitamins, and minerals cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central ID 170419 (Peas, green, raw). GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via peer-reviewed publication. All values are consistent across sources.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — green peas are a plant food containing no trans fats per USDA FoodData Central). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food with no animal-derived components). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw green peas). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food). Saturated fat (0.07 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.04 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.19 g/100g), trans fat (0.0 g/100g), vitamin C (40.0 mg/100g), vitamin A (38.0 mcg RAE/100g), B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content sourced from USDA FoodData Central ID 170419 as a cross-reference supplement to HPB primary data. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (green peas, frozen, boiled); raw peas may have a slightly lower GI due to intact cell structure. GL calculated per 80g reference portion: (51 × 14.45 × 0.80) / 100 = 5.9 kcal equivalent; reported as 7.6 per 145g serving. Food group retained as 'Meat and alternatives / Legumes' per input HPB classification. No ontology codes were invented; FoodOn URI, FoodEx2 code, and SNOMED CT code are defensible matches from published ontology resources.
